description:
You are on-call as a database-reliability engineer and have just received a clean shell on a fresh Linux box (username: “user”).  
    Standard policy says that before touching any live databases you must configure a local backup target and prove that it is recorded
    in both your shell start-up configuration and an audit log.

    Your goals (they can be accomplished with any 2–3 reasonable shell commands):

    1. Create the directory “/home/user/db_backups”.  
       • If the directory already exists, leave it in place; do not delete or rename anything.

    2. Register the directory path as an environment variable named BACKUP_DIR that is loaded automatically for interactive logins.  
       • Append the following exact text (including the surrounding double-quotes) as its own new line at the bottom of /home/user/.bashrc  
         export BACKUP_DIR="/home/user/db_backups"

    3. Produce a one-line audit log at /home/user/backup_setup.log.  
       • The log file must contain exactly the single line (no extra spaces before/after, no additional lines)
         BACKUP_DIR set to /home/user/db_backups

    Verification format requirements that the automated checker will enforce:
       a. Directory “/home/user/db_backups” must exist and be a directory.  
       b. The final non-empty line of /home/user/.bashrc must match exactly  
          export BACKUP_DIR="/home/user/db_backups"  
       c. The file /home/user/backup_setup.log must exist and its full contents must be exactly  
          BACKUP_DIR set to /home/user/db_backups

    You do not need to test the variable in a new shell; simply make sure the file edits and directory creation succeed.
truth:
Expected state after successful completion:

    1. Path /home/user/db_backups exists (mode 755 or inherited default) and is a directory.

    2. The file /home/user/.bashrc exists and its final non-empty line is the 46-character ASCII
       export BACKUP_DIR="/home/user/db_backups"

    3. The file /home/user/backup_setup.log exists with byte-exact content:
       “BACKUP_DIR set to /home/user/db_backups\n”  (i.e., 35 visible characters followed by a single newline)

    No other files need to be modified, created, or removed.
